odems.help Mar 6, 2006 CONTENTS: Network Connections from Home How to get a Home IP Account if you do not have an SID Line Settings and Terminal Types X Windows on PCs and MACs File Transfers Setting up routing from UNIX R

s File Transfers Setting up routing from UNIX Reporting Problems Network Connections from Home ----------------------------- Please see http://inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/connecting.html for current information about connecting to our computers

information about connecting to our computers from home. This document contains some troubleshooting ideas for people trying to connect via old serial-line modems and via broadband connecections. How to get a Home IP Account if you do not

ions. How to get a Home IP Account if you do not have an SID ------------------------------------------------------ Users are expected to supply their student or employee ID numbers when requesting a home-IP account. Users in groups such as CS

ting a home-IP account. Users in groups such as CS Reentry, visiting scholars and UC Extension are not issued SID numbers by the University. These user should: --> Go to 206 Evans Hall and fill out an Eligibility Application (bring wh

ill out an Eligibility Application (bring whatever proof of eligibility you have). This will allow you to set up a home-ip account for the IS&T modems. Line Settings and Terminal Types -------------------------------- If you are usin

-------------------------------- If you are using Home-IP, your dialup software will probably take care of the connection speed and terminal type settings for you. But here is information for do-it-yourselfers, such as if you are configuring

do-it-yourselfers, such as if you are configuring your own UNIX software. When connecting from a modem, use these line settings: parity: none, Data bits: 8, Stop bits: 1 or parity: even, Data bits: 7, Stop bits: 1 Most

parity: even, Data bits: 7, Stop bits: 1 Most software will work with either setting. The first one is preferable; however, some older file transfer software may only work with the second one. Once you are logged in to a UNIX account, yo

Once you are logged in to a UNIX account, you can set your terminal type if necessary using these commands (the terminal type "vt100" is used for an example): setenv TERM vt100 unsetenv TERMCAP set term=vt100 tset vt1

unsetenv TERMCAP set term=vt100 tset vt100 If that works then you might want to set up an alias to do this more easily. You could insert this line into your .login file: alias newterm 'setenv TERM \!*; unsetenv TERMCAP; set te

wterm 'setenv TERM \!*; unsetenv TERMCAP; set term=\!*' Then, once the .login file has been read (either during a login or if you run the command "source .login"), you can type "newterm vt100" to get the same result. If you login at a

0" to get the same result. If you login at a different type of terminal, you can type "newterm z19", "newterm vt220" or etc. X Windows on PCs and MACs ------------------------- X Windows ("X11") is the standard windowing system for UN

ws ("X11") is the standard windowing system for UNIX and allows windows to be displayed from one computer to another over the net. It can be installed for use via simple serial line or home-IP connections. Generally, you will not be able to

onnections. Generally, you will not be able to tolerate inter-computer performance unless your modem is at least 14.4 KB. On Windows systems, Exceed is a third-party X Windows server that is typically used. In MacOSX and other UNIX-bas

s typically used. In MacOSX and other UNIX-based systems, X Windows servers are either built-in or available for free from WEB sites. See /share/b/pub/XWindows.help for details. File Transfers -------------- The SSH File Transfer pro

ansfers -------------- The SSH File Transfer program is the best tool for doing file transfers between Instructional UNIX and Windows computers. SSH is available for free from http://inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/download-ssh.html. See http://inst

berkeley.edu/download-ssh.html. See http://inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/connecting.html#file_transfers for information about file transfers from UNIX-UNIX, UNIX-Windows and MacOS-Windows. Setting up routing from UNIX ----------------------------

up routing from UNIX ---------------------------- Users of Linux and other versions of UNIX at home can use the 'dip' program to initiate the SLIP connection. 'dip' is included with Linux. Once 'dip' is installed on your disk, you can fin

nce 'dip' is installed on your disk, you can find help in the directories /usr/docs/dip and with the command 'man dip'. Note that the "default" command found in the sample 'dip' scripts may not set up your network routing properly. It as

ot set up your network routing properly. It assumes that the system you dial into for SLIP will handle your routing, but our Annexes do not serve as routers. Instead, the Annex must pass all of your outgoing network traffic to another ga

of your outgoing network traffic to another gateway/router system. You can use 'route' to set that up. For example, let's assume these network addresses: - the Annex you dial into is "136.152.64.31" - the gateway/router address

"136.152.64.31" - the gateway/router address you were assigned is "136.152.64.1" First, run 'dip' to create your SLIP connection to the Annex. You can verify that the SLIP connection exists by typing "ifconfig" and "netstat -i" (the

sts by typing "ifconfig" and "netstat -i" (the SLIP interface is generally called "sl0"). If you don't see a SLIP interface, then 'dip' did not work. Be sure your modem is OK (you can use the "term" command in 'dip' to test basic Hayes com

he "term" command in 'dip' to test basic Hayes commands) and that you have "SLIP" compiled into your kernel (you may have the 'dmesg' command, which will display the bootup messages and should show that the SLIP driver is installed). Th

how that the SLIP driver is installed). Then, test your connection to the outside world: % ping 136.152.64.31 % ping 136.152.64.1 The first one tests the connection to the Annex, the second one tests the connection to the

nex, the second one tests the connection to the gateway/router. The first will probably work (0% packet loss is good), the second will probably not. See "man ping". You can use the 'route' command to configure your local routing table so

' command to configure your local routing table so that outgoing traffic will be sent to the gateway/router via the Annex: % route add 136.152.64.31 sl0 % route add -net 136.152.64.0 gw 136.152.64.31 sl0 See "man route" for more in

136.152.64.31 sl0 See "man route" for more info. Reporting Problems ------------------ If you have trouble logging into the UC campus modems, please send your SID, modem login name to and a description of the problem to "drmicro@so

and a description of the problem to "drmicro@socrates.berkeley.edu" (510/642-8899). Any problem report should include: - your name - your student ID or employee ID number - your home IP account name - your home IP computer name - NOT you

unt name - your home IP computer name - NOT your home IP password (insecure if send in email) - the modem number(s) you dial when the problem occurs - whether you run "SLIP" or "PPP" - when or how often the problem has occurred - a description

ow often the problem has occurred - a description of the symptoms, such as "the modems answer but my password never works"; "the modems answer and I get garbage characters"; "the modems answer and I get disconnected", etc
ms answer and I get disconnected", etc EECS Instructional Support Group 378/386 Cory Hall, 333 Soda Hall inst@eecs.berkeley.edu
